provenance.rationale: An expired credential must be treated as unauthenticated and rejected.

assertions.subject: Delete response status for expired-credential caller

observationPoint.description: Persistence check of the target task after the request

provenance.rationale: A rejected expired-credential delete must not destroy the task.

provenance.rationale: An unauthenticated delete must be rejected at the authentication layer.

observationPoint.description: Persistence check of the target task after the request

provenance.rationale: A rejected unauthenticated delete must not destroy the task.

provenance.rationale: A rejected list must not return any task data.

assertions.subject: Task data in the unauthenticated list response body

provenance.rationale: An unauthenticated list must be rejected before scoping.

assertions.note: Exact rejection code (403 vs 404) is unconfirmed; see oq-non-owner-delete-response. Asserting on a 4xx rejection until resolved.

dataRequirements.description: A task owned by a user other than the caller, used to verify non-owner and admin override deletion/listing are rejected or scoped out.
dataRequirements.name: Task owned by a different user

dataRequirements.description: A task whose owner is the authenticated caller under test, used for legitimate owner deletion and caller-scoped listing.
dataRequirements.name: Task owned by the calling user

dataRequirements.description: A task record that already exists in the system, owned by some user, used to attempt unauthenticated/expired-credential operations against a real resource.
dataRequirements.name: Existing task record

provenance.rationale: Owner-only deletion and caller-scoped listing both presuppose that addressable tasks exist; a real target task is required to exercise the rejection paths.

dataRequirements.description: A credential for a caller holding an administrative role/override, used to confirm admin privileges do not bypass owner scoping.
dataRequirements.name: Administrator caller credential

dataRequirements.description: A credential that was once valid but has passed its validity window, used to confirm expired authentication is treated as unauthenticated.
dataRequirements.name: Expired caller credential

provenance.rationale: Enforcing owner-only deletion requires a currently authenticated identity; an expired credential is needed to verify it does not establish one.

evidence.claim: There is no admin override for task deletion or listing.
Listing tasks returns only the tasks belonging to the calling user.
evidence.excerpt: listing returns only the caller's tasks

evidence.claim: Only the task owner is permitted to delete a task.
evidence.excerpt: Only a task owner may delete it

features.description: Return only tasks belonging to the calling user when listing, with no administrative role or override permitted to bypass caller-scoped listing.
features.name: Task Listing Scope

provenance.rationale: Groups the caller-scoped listing rule with the no-admin-override constraint that explicitly governs listing scope.

features.description: Restrict task deletion to the task owner, rejecting non-owner deletion requests, with no administrative role or override permitted to bypass the owner-only rule.
features.name: Task Deletion Authorization

provenance.rationale: Groups the owner-only delete rule with the no-admin-override constraint that explicitly governs deletion.

features.description: Establish an authenticated calling user identity on delete and list requests before any owner or caller scoping is enforced; anonymous requests cannot be evaluated against ownership or caller rules.
features.name: Caller Authentication

provenance.rationale: Authentication is grouped separately because it is the precondition shared by both deletion and listing scoping; owner and caller checks presuppose an identified user.

targets.description: Authentication layer that resolves the calling user identity for task delete and list requests.

warnings: [info] Coverage of the three explicit requirements is complete and non-duplicative: anonymous-rejection, owner-success, authenticated-non-owner-rejection, and no-admin-override are each exercised for both DELETE and (where applicable) GET, and every test case maps to at least one requirement. No unsupported test cases were found.

[warning] Orphaned data requirement: 'data-expired-credential' is defined but not referenced in any test case's consumesDataKeys. tc-expired-token-delete-rejected consumes only 'data-existing-task' despite requiring an expired credential. Add 'data-expired-credential' to its consumesDataKeys to restore traceability.

[warning] Orphaned data requirement: 'data-admin-credential' is defined but not referenced by tc-admin-delete-override-rejected or tc-admin-list-override-rejected (both consume only data-owner-task/data-other-user-task). The admin actor's credential provisioning is untraceable; add 'data-admin-credential' to those cases' consumesDataKeys.

[info] Asymmetric authentication coverage: an expired-credential rejection case exists for DELETE (tc-expired-token-delete-rejected) but not for GET /tasks. req-authentication-required governs listing too; listing is only negatively tested via the anonymous path. Consider a symmetric expired-credential list-rejection case.

[info] Missing edge scenarios that are reasonable but not evidence-mandated: (a) malformed/invalid (non-expired) credential rejection, distinct from expired; (b) deletion of a non-existent task id as a baseline, which interacts with the unresolved 403-vs-404 disclosure question (oq-non-owner-delete-response). These are gap candidates, not blocking omissions.

[info] Assumption embedded in assertion: as-anon-list-status and as-anon-del-status expect 401, but with oq-auth-mechanism still open a design returning an empty 200 for anonymous listing is plausible. The expectation is defensible under req-authentication-required; flagging it as an assumption tied to an unresolved open question rather than a confirmed contract.

[info] Two open questions are marked blocking:true (oq-ownership-determination, oq-auth-mechanism), but both concern implementation specifics already absorbed by partial-readiness flags and tolerant assertions (e.g. 4xx range for non-owner reject). They do not create a material coverage hole, so the review does not mark the plan incomplete.

provenance.rationale: Listing is scoped strictly to the caller's own tasks; whether sharing or assignment exists is unstated and affects scoping logic.

openQuestions.question: Can tasks ever be shared with or assigned to non-owner users, and if so, do they appear in those users' listings?

provenance.rationale: Owner and caller scoping imply authenticated identity, but no authentication scheme is described in the evidence.

openQuestions.question: What authentication mechanism identifies the calling user (session token, bearer JWT, API key)?

provenance.rationale: Enforcing owner-only deletion requires a defined source of truth for ownership, which the evidence does not describe.

openQuestions.question: How is task ownership established and recorded (e.g., the creating user is permanently the owner, or can ownership be transferred)?

provenance.rationale: The rule forbids non-owner deletion but does not specify the rejection semantics, which affects information disclosure.

openQuestions.question: What response should a non-owner deletion attempt return (403 Forbidden vs 404 Not Found to avoid leaking task existence)?

requirements.statement: Only the task owner may delete a task; deletion requests from non-owners must be rejected.

provenance.rationale: Enforcing 'owner' and 'caller' scoping presupposes an identified, authenticated user; the rules cannot be applied to anonymous requests.

requirements.statement: Requests to delete or list tasks must be associated with an authenticated calling user before owner or caller scoping can be enforced.
Listing tasks must return only tasks belonging to the calling user.
No administrative role or override may bypass owner-only deletion or caller-scoped listing rules.

steps.description: Send a DELETE request for an existing task presenting the expired credential.

provenance.rationale: An expired credential does not establish a current authenticated caller, so the delete must be rejected before ownership is checked.

action.subject: Persisted task record for the target id

steps.description: Observe the persisted state of the target task after the expired-credential request.

provenance.rationale: A rejected expired-credential delete must leave the task intact.

steps.description: Authenticated as an administrator, send a GET /tasks request.

action.subject: Task list returned in the response body

steps.description: Observe the task collection returned in the response body to the administrator.
Authenticated as the caller, send a GET /tasks request.

action.subject: Task list returned in the response body

steps.description: Observe the task collection returned in the response body.
Send a DELETE request for an existing task with no authentication credential present.

provenance.rationale: An anonymous delete must be rejected at the authentication layer before owner scoping is evaluated.

action.subject: Persisted task record for the target id

steps.description: Observe the persisted state of the target task after the unauthenticated request.

provenance.rationale: A rejected unauthenticated delete must leave the task intact.

steps.description: Send a GET /tasks request with no authentication credential present.

provenance.rationale: An anonymous list must be rejected before caller scoping is applied, so no task data can be returned.

steps.description: Authenticated as user B (non-owner), send a DELETE request for a task owned by user A.

action.subject: Persisted task record for the target id

steps.description: Observe the persisted state of the target task after the non-owner request.
Send a DELETE request for the owned task authenticated as the task owner.

action.subject: Persisted task record for the deleted id

steps.description: Observe that the previously owned task is no longer retrievable.
Authenticated as an administrator, send a DELETE request for a task owned by a regular user.

action.subject: Persisted task record for the target id

steps.description: Observe the persisted state of the target task after the administrator request.

blockers: Requires a deterministic way to produce or simulate an expired credential.

testCases.objective: Verify that a delete request presenting an expired credential is treated as unauthenticated and rejected before owner scoping.

postconditions.description: The task remains present and unmodified.

preconditions.description: A task exists and a credential that has passed its validity window is available.

provenance.rationale: An expired credential does not establish a current authenticated caller, so owner-only deletion cannot be enforced and the request must be rejected.

testCases.riskRationale: Accepting expired credentials would let formerly valid sessions delete tasks without a currently authenticated identity.
testCases.title: Reject task deletion when caller authentication is expired

testCases.objective: Verify that a caller holding an administrative role or override receives only their own tasks when listing, not all users' tasks.

postconditions.description: Response contains only the administrator's own tasks.
No other user's tasks appear despite admin role.

preconditions.description: Multiple users own tasks; the authenticated caller is an administrator owning a subset.

testCases.riskRationale: An admin override on listing would expose every user's tasks, defeating the caller-scoping confidentiality guarantee.
testCases.title: Administrative role cannot bypass caller-scoped listing

blockers: Treatment of shared tasks in caller-scoped listing is unconfirmed (see oq-shared-task-access).

testCases.objective: Verify that an authenticated caller listing tasks receives only tasks they own and never tasks owned by other users.

postconditions.description: Response contains only the caller's tasks.
No task owned by another user appears in the response.

preconditions.description: The caller owns at least one task and at least one task is owned by a different user.

testCases.riskRationale: Leaking other users' tasks in a list response is a direct confidentiality breach of caller-scoped data.
testCases.title: Listing returns only the calling user's tasks

testCases.objective: Verify that a delete request lacking an authenticated calling user identity is rejected before owner scoping is evaluated.

postconditions.description: The task remains present and unmodified.
Request is rejected with an unauthenticated/authentication-required outcome.

preconditions.description: A task exists in the system.

provenance.rationale: Owner-only deletion presupposes an identified caller; an anonymous delete must be rejected at the authentication layer before ownership is checked.

testCases.riskRationale: If unauthenticated deletes are processed, owner scoping cannot be enforced and any task could be destroyed by an anonymous caller.
testCases.title: Reject task deletion from anonymous (unauthenticated) caller

testCases.objective: Verify that a list request lacking an authenticated calling user identity is rejected before caller scoping is evaluated.

postconditions.description: No task data is returned in the response body.
Request is rejected with an authentication-required outcome.

preconditions.description: At least one task belonging to some user exists.

provenance.rationale: Caller-scoped listing presupposes an identified caller; an anonymous list must be rejected at the authentication layer before scoping is applied.

testCases.riskRationale: Without an identified caller, listing cannot be scoped and could expose tasks belonging to other users.
testCases.title: Reject task listing from anonymous (unauthenticated) caller

blockers: Expected rejection response for non-owner delete is unconfirmed (see oq-non-owner-delete-response).

testCases.objective: Verify that an authenticated caller who does not own a task cannot delete it and the task is preserved.

postconditions.description: The task remains present and unmodified.

preconditions.description: A task is owned by user A; the caller is authenticated user B who does not own it.

testCases.riskRationale: Allowing non-owners to delete tasks is a direct authorization bypass enabling destruction of other users' data.
testCases.title: Reject deletion of a task by an authenticated non-owner

testCases.objective: Verify that an authenticated caller who owns a task can delete it and the task is removed.

postconditions.description: The owned task no longer exists.

preconditions.description: An authenticated user owns an existing task.

testCases.riskRationale: Core deletion authorization path; a failure here blocks legitimate owners and indicates the authorization check is misconfigured.
testCases.title: Task owner successfully deletes own task

testCases.objective: Verify that a caller holding an administrative role or override cannot delete a task they do not own.

postconditions.description: The task remains present and unmodified.

preconditions.description: A task is owned by a regular user; the caller is an authenticated administrator who does not own it.

testCases.riskRationale: An admin override path would silently defeat the owner-only deletion guarantee for every task in the system.
testCases.title: Administrative role cannot override owner-only deletion
